data.title: Constellation Energy: A Growth Opportunity Amid Volatility (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Constellation Energy Corporation's (NASDAQ:CEG) stock price was $254.98/share at my last review on September 22, 2024, and its market capitalization at that time was $79.7 billion. At close on April 8, 2025, Constellation’s stock price was -27.5% lower at $184.94/share and market capitalization was similarly lower at $57.9 billion. By comparison, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) is down -16.3% from its high.

Constellation is the top US producer of carbon-free electricity with a 10% share of the country’s carbon-free electricity via its nuclear, wind, solar, and hydro units. It is notable for having the overall largest nuclear fleet in the US.

However, with a tiny 0.84% dividend, I do not recommend Constellation Energy to dividend investors.

For longer-term growth, capital appreciation, and momentum investors interested in energy and who are comfortable with the equity market’s recent tariff-driven high volatility, I am up-ranking Constellation Energy from hold to buy because its price drop has made Constellation more affordable. The underlying thesis continues: Constellation supplies needed nuclear base load electricity to the hungry PJM grid and other regional grids at a time when a) supply reliability is ever more important and b) electricity demand is expected to increase at a faster rate than previously due to growth of artificial intelligence data centers. This can be seen in Constellation’s recent contract with Microsoft (MSFT) to restart the undamaged nuclear unit at Three Mile Island (renamed the Crane Clean Energy Center), expected to occur in spring 2028.

Another notable development, described below, is Constellation’s proposed $26.6 billion acquisition of Calpine, a large natural gas-fueled electricity generator. Because new nuclear generation units can take several years to build, natural gas generation has become more highly valued as a readily available solution to meet data center demand growth now. Additional natural gas generation can be built quickly and while it can operate as base load, gas-fired generation is also quick on/quick off, useful for backing up renewables.

On December 31, 2024, Constellation owned 31,700 megawatts of electricity generating capacity. Within this, Constellation’s nuclear-powered capacity is 22,100 megawatts, or 70% of the total.

The illustrations below show Constellation’s generating resources by type and location. On the map:

  • Light blue is nuclear
  • Dark blue is hydro
  • Purple is natural gas/other
  • Light green is wind
  • Orange is solar

I own shares of Constellation Energy.

Proposed Acquisition of Calpine

On January 10, 2025, Constellation announced it was acquiring private electricity generator Calpine for $26.6 billion, comprised of equity at $16.4 billion (50 million shares of Constellation stock plus $4.5 billion in cash), assumption of $12.7 billion in Calpine’s debt, and the total offset somewhat by tax benefits.

Together, Constellation and Calpine would have 60 gigawatts of generating capacity from nuclear, natural gas, and renewables. Calpine alone has 27.7 GW of capacity in 79 plants and is one of the largest US owners of gas-generated electricity plants. This includes:

  • 61 combined cycle gas turbines with 26 GW of capacity and 425 MW under construction
  • 13 operating geothermal plants with a capacity of 725 MW
  • Solar and battery storage assets with 740 MW of capacity and 160 MW under construction
  • 6000 MW of co-generation

Calpine's locations are illustrated below.

Together, their asset footprint and service territory would be larger, with deeper presence in Texas (ERCOT market grid), Pennsylvania, California (CAISO market grid), and the mid-Atlantic (PJM market grid).

As Constellation points out in its investor presentation, because most new generation supply queued up and awaiting interconnection is intermittent renewables, there is a competitive advantage in offering base load and 24/7 reliable supply from sources like nuclear and natural gas.

Combined, Constellation and Calpine would offer the largest clean energy generation output at 308 million megawatt-hours (MWhs), followed by NextEra Energy at 228 million MWhs, Duke at 216 million MWhs, and Vistra at 206 million MWhs.

The transaction is expected to close within twelve months of the announcement. Major Calpine shareholders ECP, Canada Pension Plan Investments, and Access Industries, agreed to an 18-month lockup, subject to an agreed-upon schedule for potential sales. According to Constellation, regulatory approvals are required from “the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, the Canadian Competition Bureau, the New York Public Service Commission, the Public Utility Commission of Texas and other regulatory agencies.”

Combined Constellation-Calpine facilities map, should the transaction close, is shown below.

Constellation 2024 Results and Guidance

In 2024, Constellation reported net income of $11.89/share or $3.75 billion. This compares to $5.01/share or $1.62 billion in 2023.

Non-GAAP adjusted operating earnings for 2024 were $8.67/share or $2.74 billion, compared to $6.28/share or $2.03 billion in 2023.

The biggest share of the difference between net income and non-GAAP adjusted operating earnings was a subtraction of $1.03 billion for the gain on fair value adjustments.

According to the year-end and Q4 2024 financial report, “Nuclear operating capacity factor was 94.6% and 94.4% for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, and 2023, respectively.”

The company reprised the news of its 20-year power purchase agreement with Microsoft that will enable it to restart the undamaged 835-MW nuclear unit (Unit 1) of Three Mile Island/Crane Clean Energy Center in Pennsylvania. Constellation anticipates investing $1.6 billion for the restart.

It completed $1 billion of share repurchases during 2024 and has $1 billion of repurchase authority remaining.

Constellation also raised its adjusted operating earnings (non-GAAP) guidance for 2025 to $8.90-$9.60/share.

In addition to its announced Calpine acquisition, Constellation has noted it is investing about $100 million to upgrade critical electrical systems and plant equipment at the Calvert Cliffs Clean Energy Center in Lusby, Maryland. Calvert Cliffs contains two nuclear units totaling 1720 MW of capacity.

Macro

Uncertainty over the ultimate size of and effects of tariffs continues to roil the US stock market and feed into concern about an economic slowdown or recession.

Near-term, the Federal Reserve is expected to keep the Fed Funds rate steady, neither increasing nor decreasing.

Still, excitement continues, as detailed by RBN Energy in a recent blog, over the tech industry’s very large need for 24/7 electricity to run artificial data centers.

Even the US Department of Energy has become involved, identifying sixteen sites on DOE land for potential co-location of data centers and new energy infrastructure. This is responsive to two January 2025 presidential executive orders titled Removing Barriers to American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence and Unleashing American Energy.

Security of supply for electricity generally has become an issue. With many base load coal plants taken offline or dismantled completely, nuclear power is increasingly seen as the vital replacement. Regulators in the PJM region, as shown in the map below, are particularly concerned the region may not have enough base load electricity capacity.

More recently, excitement has increased about the use of natural gas for generation of electricity, as generous amounts of gas power plants already exist, and new ones can be quickly built. However, investors should note that another big new and competitive source of US gas demand, in addition to data centers, is for LNG export.

Competitors

Constellation Energy is headquartered in Baltimore, Maryland.

As noted above, its acquisition of Calpine will make it the largest producer of energy from natural gas.

CEG competes with utilities that have nuclear power production such as The Southern Company (SO), Duke (DUK), Tennessee Valley Authority, Dominion Energy (D), Public Service Enterprise (PEG), NextEra Energy (NEE), and Entergy (ETR).

It also competes with other independent power producers like Vistra (VST).

Efforts are additionally underway to restart the Palisades nuclear plant in Michigan (owned by private company Holtec) and Iowa’s Duane Arnold nuclear plant, owned by NextEra.

In all, the US has 94 operating nuclear reactors at 54 plants. In 2023, US nuclear generated 18.6% of US electricity.

As the graph below shows, nuclear generation is highest in summer and winter.

Governance

On April 1, 2025, Institutional Shareholder Services ranked Constellation Energy’s overall governance as 6, with sub-scores of audit (3), board (4), shareholder rights (9), and compensation (3). In this ranking, a 1 indicates lower governance risk and a 10 indicates higher governance risk.

On March 13, 2025, shorts were 2.9% of float. Insiders owned a small 0.25% of the company’s equity.

The company’s beta has risen from 0.73 at the last report to 1.02, or now similar to the volatility of the market overall.

At December 31, 2024, the five largest institutional stockholders, some of which represent index fund investments that match the overall market, were Vanguard (11.9%), Capital International (8.9%), BlackRock (7.5%), State Street (5.6%), and Fidelity/FMR (4.6%).

Financial and Stock Highlights For Constellation Energy

On April 8, 2025, Constellation’s closing stock price was $184.94/share for a market capitalization of $57.9 billion.

With a 52-week price range of $155.60-$352.00/share, the closing price is 53% of the high and 58% of the one-year target of $319.71/share.

Trailing twelve months’ (TTM) return on assets is 5.15% while TTM return on equity is 30.1%.

TTM earnings per share (EPS) were $11.88 for a price-earnings ratio of 15.6. The averages of analysts’ current 2025 and 2026 EPS estimates are $9.45 and $10.89 respectively, for a forward price-earnings ratio range of 17.0-19.6.

In the last three quarters, Constellation Energy beat by the averages of analysts’ expectations by $0.79 in Q2 2024, $1.00 in Q3 2024, and $0.52 in Q4 2024.

TTM operating cash flow is -$2.46 billion. TTM levered free cash flow is $4.23 billion.

Prior to the announcement of the acquisition of Calpine, on December 31, 2024, the company had $39.8 billion in liabilities, including $7.4 billion of long-term debt and $52.9 billion in assets, giving Constellation a steep liability-to-asset ratio of 75%.

Constellation’s dividend is $1.55/year for a yield of 0.84%.

The average analyst rating for is 2.3, or “buy,” leaning toward “hold,” from sixteen analysts.

Positive and Negative Risks

The overarching negative risk remains high market volatility due to tariff uncertainties.

Investors should consider their expectations for base load clean electricity demand growth, as well as total electricity demand growth, as the factor most likely to affect Constellation. Artificial intelligence data center growth has jump-started these expectations, a positive risk.

Nuclear has grid planning characteristics and advantages over renewables because it is base load, also a positive risk.

A negative risk is that Constellation’s acquisition of Calpine may run into delays or may be completely forestalled by regulators. And, even should the acquisition go through, given higher current natural gas prices, the margin between gas price costs and electricity sales revenues may shrink.

Recommendations for Constellation Energy

I don’t recommend Constellation Energy to dividend-hunting investors.

The company has the competitive advantage of being the dominant nuclear electricity supplier in the US. Assuming it is approved to complete the acquisition of Calpine, it will also add major gas generation capacity, nearly doubling its generation capacity to 60 GW.

Because of the general stock market downdraft over tariffs, virtually all stock prices have fallen initially as investors sort through the consequences. While Constellation is exposed to market volatility, the all-sectors price reduction presents an opportunity to buy this growth stock in a growth sector at a more affordable price.

I recommend Constellation Energy as a buy to growth-seeking investors due to the big need for reliable (base load) power from grid-stable generation for AI data centers and the general need for electricity reliability provided investors are comfortable with the general stock market’s large drop and continuing extreme volatility over tariffs.

I own shares of Constellation Energy.
